sections.paragraph:
πρίν (Hom.+; ins, pap, LXX) lit. an adv. of time ‘before, formerly’, then a marker of a point of time prior to another point of time, before

ⓐ as temporal conjunction (the ms. tradition oft. varies betw. πρίν, which is predom. Attic, and πρὶν ἤ [s. ἤ 2d], which is Ionic [Schwyzer II 313], and predominates in the Koine [Schwyzer II 656]).

α. w. the aor. subj. (Plut., Caes. 735 [58, 2]; Lucian, Ver. Hist. 2, 18) or opt. (B-D-F §383, 3; Rob. 977) πρὶν ἀκουσθῶσι τὰ ῥήματα Hs 5, 7, 3. Lk 2:26 is text-critically uncertain πρὶν ἢ ἂν ἴδῃ, but ἤ is omitted (cp. Cyr.-Ins. 123 πρὶν ἄν w. aor. subj.) as well as ἄν in some mss., and one v.l. has ἕως ἂν ἴδῃ. Likew. in 22:34 ἕως, ἕως οὗ, ἕως ὅτου are also attested instead of πρὶν ἤ; the corrector has been at work in all these passages, so that the original rdg. can no longer be determined w. certainty.—Only once w. the opt., in indirect discourse after a past tense (B-D-F §386, 4; Rob. 970) πρὶν ἢ ὁ κατηγορούμενος ἔχοι Ac 25:16 (cp. Jos., Ant. 20, 210).

β. foll. by the acc. and the aor. inf. (B-D-F §395; Rob. 977.—Plut., Lysander 448 [27, 1] πρὶν ἐπανελθεῖν τὸν Ἀγησίλαον; Lucian, Dial. Deor. 20, 16 πρίν; Jos., Ant. 11, 1 πρὶν ἤ) Mt 1:18 (πρὶν ἤ); J 8:58; Ac 7:2 (πρὶν ἤ); 1 Cl 38:3: Dg 2:3 (πρὶν ἤ); 8:1; Hs 9, 16, 3. Also of future things (Arrian, Ind. 24, 6 πρὶν ταχθῆναι τὴν φάλαγγα=before the phalanx will have been drawn up) πρὶν ἀλέκτορα φωνῆσαι before the cock will have crowed Mt 26:34, 75; Mk 14:30 (πρὶν ἤ), 72; Lk 22:61. Cp. J 4:49; Ac 2:20 (Jo 3:4); Hs 9, 26, 6.—Without the acc., which is understood fr. the context (Menand., Epitr. 264 S. [88 Kö.]; 508 S. [332 Kö.]; Diod S 13, 10, 1; 14, 52, 1; Chion, Ep. 4, 4; Ps.-Apollod. 3, 3, 2; Jos., Bell. 6, 213; Just., A I, 4, 4) J 14:29; Hv 3, 1, 3.

ⓑ funct. as prep. w. gen. (since Pind., P. 4, 43, also Plut., Mor. 883b; Arrian, Anab. 3, 18, 6; PGM 7, 418; 420 πρὶν ἡλίου ἀνατολῆς; En 14:6; Jos., Ant. 4, 269 πρὶν ἡλίου δυσμῶν; Sus 35a LXX=42 Theod.; for contrast w. πρότερον cp. the epitath οὐ τὸ θανεῖν ἄλγεινόν, ἐπεὶ τό γε Μοῖρʼ ἐπέκλωσεν, | ἀλλὰ πρὶν ἡλικίας καὶ γονέων πρότερον EpigrAnat 13, ’89, 128f, no. 2=dying is not itself to be deplored, for Fate decrees it so, but when before one comes of age and then before one’s parents) πρὶν ἀλεκτοροφωνίας Mt 26:34 v.l.; πρὶν Ἀβραὰμ ἐγὼ εἰμί J 8:58 v.l.—Even πρίν w. the acc. occurs as v.l. πρὶν τὸ πάσχα J 11:55 D; πρὶν σάββατον Mk 15:42 D (cp. B-D-F §395 app.; JWackernagel, Syntax II2 1928, 215).—ATschuschke, De πρίν particulae apud scriptores aet. August. prosaicos usu, diss. Bresl. 1913.—DELG. M-M.

Hom Hom , VIII b.c.—List 5

ins Ins, ins = Inscription, Inschrift, inscription(s). Without a period, esp. in lists, as at the beginning of entries; the capitalized form is used in titles. In conjunction with literary works this abbr. refers to the title or description of contents.

pap pap = papyrus, -yri

LXX LXX = Septuaginta, ed. ARahlfs, unless otherwise specified—Lists 2, beg.

lit. lit. = literal(ly); literature (refererences to [scholarly] literature)

adv. adv. = adverb, adverbially

  1. ms. = manuscript(s)

oft. oft. = often

betw. betw. = between

predom. predom. = predominant(ly)

Schwyzer Schwyzer = ES., Griechische Grammatik—List 6

aor. aor. = aorist

subj. subj. = subjunctive; subject

Plut Plut , I–II a.d.—List 5

Lucian Lucian , II a.d.—List 5

Hist.

Hist. (after a personal name)=historicus, historian

hist. = historian, historical

opt. opt. = optative

B-D-F B-D-F = FBlass, ADebrunner, RFunk, A Greek Grammar of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature—List 6

Rob. Rob. = ATRobertson, A Grammar of the Greek NT in the Light of Historical Research—List 6

Hs Hs = Similitudes

  1. cp. = compare, freq. in ref. to citation fr. ancient texts

v.l. v.l. = varia lectio (variant reading)

rdg. rdg. = reading(s)

Jos. Jos. = Josephus. This abbr. used when follow by title; I a.d.—Lists 5

foll. foll. = followed, following

acc. acc. = accusative

inf. inf. = infinitive

1 Cl 1 Cl = 1 Clement—List 1

Dg Dg = Letter of Diognetus, attributed to an unidentifiable apologist; II a.d.—List 1

Arrian Arrian = Flavius Arrianus, I–II a.d.—List 5

  1. fr. = from

Menand Menand , various dramas cited; IV–III b.c.—List 5

Diod S Diod S , I b.c.—List 5

Chion Chion , IV b.c.; for letters attributed to him, I a.d., s. Ep.—List 5

Ep.

Ep. = Epistola/Epistula, when applied to letters mostly pseudonymous; various dates—List 5

  1. = epistle

Ps.-Apollod Ps.-Apollod , myth., I/II a.d.—List 5

Just Just , II a.d.—List 5

Hv Hv = Visions

prep. prep. = preposition(al)

gen. gen. = genitive

Pind Pind , V b.c.—List 5

PGM PGM = Papyri Graecae Magicae—List 4

En En = I Enoch—List 2

Theod. Theod. = Theodotion, revision of Greek OT, II a.d.—List 2, beg.

EpigrAnat EpigrAnat = Epigraphica Anatolica—List 3

D D = Didache, except that in a list of manuscripts or as textual variant D refers to Codex Bezae—List 1

app. app. = appendix, apparatus (when ref. to marginal text-critical information, esp. in N.)

diss. diss. = dissertation

DELG DELG = PChantraine, Dictionnaire étymologique de la langue grecque—List 6

M-M M-M = JMoulton/GMilligan, Vocabulary of Greek Testament—Lists 4, 6

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 863
